title: Managing a doe from kindle to weaning
subtitle: The six weeks from birth to independence for a meat-rabbit doe and her litter.
excerpt: The first 48 hours after kindling are the most critical in a rabbit's life. Kits that miss the doe's first feed do not survive. This tutorial covers nest preparation before kindling, the first-morning check, feeding during lactation, and weaning at five to six weeks without disrupting the colony.
sourceNotes:
DEFRA Code of Recommendations for the Welfare of Rabbits (2009, Crown copyright). https://www.gov.uk/government/collections/animal-welfare-codes-of-practice
SAC Consulting Scotland, Rabbit production factsheets.
Carla Emery, Encyclopedia of Country Living (Sasquatch Books, 12th ed. 2012), rabbit chapter. Referenced for management cycle.

glossaryTerms.definition: The act of giving birth in rabbits. A doe kindles a litter; the kits are born blind, deaf, and hairless. She lines the nest box with fur pulled from her own belly before birth.
Newborn and young rabbits from birth to weaning. At birth, kits weigh 40 to 60 grams and have no fur. They open their eyes at around ten days.
An adult female rabbit. A doe reaches breeding maturity at 4 to 6 months depending on the breed. She suckles her kits for one feeding bout per day, usually in the early morning.
The period during which the doe produces milk for her kits, typically four to six weeks. Rabbit milk is one of the richest of any mammal; a single daily feed is enough to sustain rapid growth.

content.text: A
doe
managing her litter alone rarely needs much intervention. What she needs is a clean nest box before she
kindles
, water and pellets available at all times once the
kits
are born, and a keeper who checks daily but does not disturb the nest unnecessarily.
Before kindling: setting up the nest box

Place a nest box in the cage around day 28 of gestation. A standard nest box for a medium meat breed is roughly 30 cm wide, 40 cm deep, and 20 cm tall, with one open end. Fill it loosely with clean meadow hay to around half depth. The doe will rearrange it.

From around day 27, the doe will begin pulling fur from her chest and belly to line the nest. This is normal. A doe that is not fur-pulling at day 30 may be a first-time breeder unused to the process; she will usually manage without help, but watch the first morning after birth carefully.
The first-morning check

Check the nest within 12 hours of kindling. Lift the nest box quietly and look at the kits. They should be in a tight group, warm to the touch, and have plump, rounded bellies. A flat, cold belly means the kit has not fed. A kit with a wrinkled skin over its flanks is dehydrated.

The doe feeds her kits once a day, usually in the early morning before you arrive. If a kit has not fed by the next morning, it needs intervention. Hold the doe on her back in your lap and place the kit on her nipple. Hold it in place for a few minutes. Some does will accept this; others will not. A kit that has missed two feeds will not survive.

attrs.title: Handling newborn kits
attrs.body: Handle kits from day one. The domestic rabbit does not abandon kits that smell of human hands; this is a myth carried over from wild rabbits. Regular handling from the first week produces calmer animals at weaning and makes the six-week development period easier to manage. Rub your hands with used bedding before the first handling session if the doe is nervous.

content.text: Feeding through lactation
A lactating doe uses two to three times her normal food intake. Do not restrict her feed during
lactation
. Keep the pellet hopper full and the water supply clean and accessible at all times. A doe that runs short of water reduces milk output within hours.

At around day 18, the kits will begin leaving the nest box to nibble at pellets and hay. Make sure the feed trough is accessible from floor level. Once kits are eating solid food, their growth accelerates and milk becomes a supplement rather than their sole nutrition.
Weaning at five to six weeks

Wean at five weeks for a fast-turnover meat-rabbit operation; six weeks is better for colony health and kit condition. At weaning, remove the doe rather than the kits. Taking the kits out of the familiar cage and putting them in a new one adds environmental stress on top of the social stress of weaning. Leave the kits in their own cage for at least a week before moving them.
Watch the doe for mastitis in the days after weaning: a hard, hot, or discoloured mammary gland needs attention. In summer, check the doe and the kits for
flystrike
daily; lactating does and young kits with soiled fur are the highest-risk animals.

items.symptom: Two or three dead kits in the nest on the first morning
items.cause: Small or weak kits pushed to the edge of the group and chilled overnight; or dam not producing milk on day one
items.fix: Remove the dead kits immediately. Count the survivors and check that those remaining are warm and fed. If the surviving kits are cold and unfed, attempt a supervised nursing session with the doe held on her back.

items.symptom: Doe aggressive toward kits after handling
items.cause: First-time doe unsettled by unfamiliar scent
items.fix: Rub the kits with used nest bedding before returning them. Keep handling brief for the first week. Some first-time does settle after a few days; a doe that continues to attack kits consistently is unlikely to improve.

items.symptom: Kits falling behind in growth after week three
items.cause: Large litter competing for limited milk; doe losing condition
items.fix: Ensure pellet and water supply is unrestricted for the doe. If the litter is larger than eight kits, introduce solid food actively from day 18 rather than waiting for the kits to find it. A small quantity of fresh meadow hay alongside pellets helps the transition.
